title: Punjabi Baingan Bharta

subs.name: if you have an oven, roast at 220°C for 30–35 minutes, but the smoky depth will be less pronounced
1.5 tsp ginger-garlic paste as a quick alternative
half a tsp of red chilli flakes at the tempering stage

steps.text: Brush the brinjals lightly with oil and place them directly on the gas flame, turning with tongs every 2–3 minutes until the skin is charred and blackened all over and the brinjal collapses when pressed — about 12–15 minutes total. Transfer to a plate to cool.

Once cool enough to handle, peel and discard the charred skin. Mash the smoky pulp roughly with a fork — do not over-mash; some texture is pleasant. Discard the stem. If it is very watery, drain the excess liquid.
Heat oil in a kadai over medium flame. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Add the onion and fry until golden brown, about 6 minutes.

Add the ginger, garlic and green chilli; cook, stirring, until the raw smell lifts, about 2 minutes. Add the tomatoes with a pinch of salt and cook until they completely break down and oil separates at the edges, about 5 minutes.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder and stir for 30 seconds. Tip in the mashed brinjal and mix everything together thoroughly over medium flame.

Cook the bharta, stirring frequently, for 5–7 minutes until the mixture comes together and any excess moisture evaporates. Add garam masala, taste for salt, and finish with a generous scatter of fresh coriander. Serve with roti, makki di roti or rice.

substitution_notes: brinjal → if you have an oven, roast at 220°C for 30–35 minutes, but the smoky depth will be less pronounced; garlic → 1.5 tsp ginger-garlic paste as a quick alternative; green-chilli → half a tsp of red chilli flakes at the tempering stage
